title: Pierogi z owocami
subtitle: Polish sweet fruit dumplings
excerpt: Soft dumplings filled with blueberries or sour cherries, boiled until just cooked through, and served warm with soured cream and sugar. A summer dessert or sweet lunch dish across Poland.
sourceNotes: Sweet fruit pierogi (pierogi z owocami) are a summer staple in Poland, made when blueberries, strawberries, or sour cherries are in season. They can be served as a dessert or, in the Polish tradition, as a main course at lunch. The dough is the same as for savoury pierogi; only the filling and accompaniments change. The fruit is used raw — cooking beforehand makes it too wet.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze assembled, uncooked, on a tray. Cook from frozen, adding 2 minutes to boiling time. The fruit releases juice during freezing and thawing — expect the filling to be softer.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Shape and freeze the surplus uncooked.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be made and rested, then refrigerated for up to 4 hours before rolling. Assemble just before cooking.

content.text: Fruit pierogi use the same dough as savoury ones, but the filling is raw fruit — no cooking, no sweetening before assembly. The fruit softens and releases its juice during boiling, and the slight tartness of blueberries or sour cherries is part of what makes the dumpling work against the soured cream and sugar at the table.

The critical point is keeping the filling small: two or three blueberries, or one stoned cherry per dumpling, with enough dough around them to seal cleanly. Too much fruit and the juice punctures the dough during boiling.

Mix the {{plain-flour}}, {{eggs}}, {{soured-cream}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} until a dough forms. Knead for 5 minutes until smooth and no longer sticky. Wrap and rest for 30 minutes at room temperature.

Mix the {{blueberries}} with 1 tablespoon of {{granulated-sugar}} and set aside. Roll the dough out to 2 mm on a lightly floured surface. Cut rounds using an 8 cm cutter. Place 3 to 4 blueberries in the centre of each round, avoiding excess juice. Fold and press firmly to seal. Place on a floured tray and do not stack.

Bring a large pot of lightly salted water to a gentle boil. Cook the pierogi in batches of 8 — they are done 2 minutes after floating to the surface. Remove with a slotted spoon and toss immediately in the melted {{unsalted-butter}}. Serve in bowls with a generous spoonful of {{soured-cream}} and a scattering of {{granulated-sugar}} over the top.

items.cause: Too much fruit inside, or fruit too juicy
items.fix: Use a small amount of fruit per dumpling — no more than 3 to 4 blueberries. Press the seal very firmly. Pat fruit dry before using if it is very wet

items.cause: Over-kneaded or not rested long enough
items.fix: Fruit pierogi need a slightly softer dough than savoury ones. Rest for the full 30 minutes and do not over-roll — 2 mm is correct

items.cause: Tossed in butter too slowly after boiling
items.fix: Have the butter melted and ready before the dumplings come out of the water. Toss immediately to coat each one

content.text: Sweet pierogi are summer food in Poland: served as a main course at lunch, or as a dessert after dinner, whenever blueberries, strawberries, or sour cherries are at their cheapest and best. The same dough, the same boiling, the same soured cream — only the filling changes. This version with blueberries is the most common; sour cherry pierogi (pierogi z wiśniami) are considered more refined and are often served at special occasions.
